sentences.text: Right.
Yes, well, the ghetto was divided-- Ghetto A and Ghetto B.
Ghetto A was for the old people, sick people, and the children.
You know, what they didn't have work for them.
They didn't have to go to work, but it was very crowded.
People were sleeping in the street.
And when we came in, as several hundred people, of course it was so much more crowded.
We got one corner in a street over there.
And that one corner was a place only with one bed.
On the one bed, nine people had to sleep.
So we slept on the floor, we slept a little bit there, little bit there.
It was very, very crowded.
But on top of that, it was very dirty, that ghetto.
And there was no food whatsoever.
Pardon me?
It was fenced in, yes with a big, high fence.
But as I was over there one day, or two days, and the-- the-- the hunger start creeping up on us, I said, I have to do something-- because usually I did.
I start walking around the fence from the Ghetto B, because the Ghetto B had already got [INAUDIBLE] their food.
The people in the Ghetto B were going outside to work different factories.
And when they worked at different factories, of course, they had food to eat.
They had-- in the evening, they had a kitchen, but the kitchen cooked very good soups for them.
And when they came back from work, they went and got the soup.
So I wanted to get over there.
Finally, I found one space over there where the fence was a little bit lower, where I was able to jump over.
When I jumped over, I started walking around in the streets.
And again, I didn't have any permission to be in the Ghetto B.
If a Gestapo would find me, I will be shot immediately.
Even if the Jewish police would have found me, you know, I don't think that they would have let me go.
They maybe would put me back or beat me up or whatever.
But I had to be very discreet and to watch myself.
When I seen over there an elderly lady, I'd ask her if she has something to eat for me.
She said, well, she has a little bit, but how much can she give me?
And I told her, I will do the work for you.
Maybe you need wood to chop, to sweep, or whatever.
I wanted to work.
Just would you give me something to eat, because in the Ghetto A, I have friends.
They're hungry.
And I would like to, if I could, get something to eat to share with them.
And she liked that very much when I said I want to share with my friends, that I'm not selfish.
So she took me into the house, and she can give me over there-- I remember, you know, a little bit milk [INAUDIBLE] with a little piece of bread.
And she gave me another couple pieces of bread that I put in the pockets for my friends.
And she, right away, went to the neighbors, you know, and start telling about here I am, you know, and I'm looking for work if they can give me work.
And I got some work in that house, in that house, In all, I got a few pieces bread or whatever I could get in my pockets and to take it on the other side.
Because I want you to know a piece of bread when you're hungry, no matter how little a piece of bread it is, that's life.
That is-- you know, when you're hungry, and you have something to put into your stomach, no matter how little it is, it is good to have it.
So whatever I could, I did, and I shared with my friends.
But that wasn't enough.
So I start looking again.
And finally, [INAUDIBLE] on the kitchen that was over there.
I went in.
I was so gutsy that I went into the kitchen and went in to the manager from the kitchen, and I asked him if he can help me, because I want to help my friends that were hungry.
We came from a camp.
So I told him the whole story.

And because I told him that I want to help my friends, but I didn't tell him that I want to help myself, he says, you know what will happen if a Gestapo man or SS man will come in the kitchen will find you, but you have no permission to be here.
I said, yes, I know what would happen, but I was so many times already in so many dangerous things, I take my chances.
This is the only way I can survive.
And he left.
He says, well, it not only will happen to you.
It will happen to me, too.
But it's worth to try it.
And he gave me a job to peel potatoes, to peel all of them and to clean and things like this.
And I start working.
And I always, you know, did more than they asked for.
I always was a very hard worker.
And-- the soup that they cooked, in the evenings when we give out the soup, always is left over in the-- in the big tubs, to clean them out.
And that soup, what I cleaned out on the tubs, I took it over to the other ghetto and shared with all my friends.
That helped a lot, because I was able to take over quite a bit.
And on top of that, you know, something like that goes around very fast.
And all the cooks, you know, they found out who I am, what I'm doing.
And I was so helping them to clean and to do-- I always was so eager to work that everybody liked me.
And that put up more to cook.
[INAUDIBLE] The leftovers would be more than average.
And I had lots of leftovers to take it over and fed the people in the other ghetto.
Finally, that Ghetto A, a sickness broke out-- the typhus.
And of course, there was no hospitals.
There was one little hospital outside the ghetto.
Only the prominent people could go in over there.
Average people, there was no room for them.
So people were dying like flies.
And sure enough, my friends got to be sick.
They got to be sick.
I said-- went to the manager, and I told him that my friends in the ghetto over there are sick, or he can help them.
He said, well, how many you have?
I said, at that time, I had four friends that got sick.
He called up.
He said a taxi can enter the ghetto, and I was there, picked them up, put them in the taxi, and put them in the hospital in.
But I was able to help.
But a few days later, maybe a week later, I got sick.
But I am a fighter.
When I get sick, I don't just lay down.
I was-- I was still working.
I was working [INAUDIBLE].
I was sweating.
I had fever.
But I was working.
One day, I picked up-- picked up a water, and I just fell flat on my face with that tub of water.
And they picked me up, and they put me in the hospital over there.
I was ten days over there till I came to myself, you know, that I start asking what's happened, where I am, because I didn't even know what's happened.
And they told me, because I've seen over there, friends what I know.
They told me what's happened.
And I was another few days over there.
I got well.
And I came back to work.
I came back to work.
They all was very happy that I got well because that sickness killed a lot of people.
This is a very, very bad sickness.
And when you get out of it, you're very lucky.
Especially with me, where I had it for so long, and I didn't say nothing, it's even worse.
Because if somebody catches, you know, in the beginning, it's easier to cure.
But with me, it was a different story.
But thank God, when I worked a few days, or a couple weeks out there, I went in the streets over there.
The Gestapo were catching all the young men they found in the street and put us to jail.
And I was caught, put in jail.
In jail, we were sitting, say, about two, three days.
Later, they picked us up, put on a truck, and to a concentration camp.
And that was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a camp between [POLISH].
[INAUDIBLE].
Till now, I was in labor camps.
But that was the first concentration camp.
The name was [?
Szebnie. ?]
That was not far from Krakow.
That was [INAUDIBLE] this is a-- a part of the ghetto of the-- what was the movie made?
Plaszow-- from Plaszow-- this was a part of Plaszow.
The same commandant, Commandant Goeth, he was in charge of that camp, too.
Did you see the movie-- what was it?
You remember that Commandant Goeth?
He was in Plaszow?
What I know about him I wish I wouldn't know.
He was a bad person, a very bad person.
I have bad names to call him, but I don't want to call him because I respect you as a lady, the names that I would call him, what he was.
What he did to people, it is just unbelievable.
And for his own enjoyment, he'd murder people.
He'd torture people, especially ladies.
He picked a young woman, and he tortured them.
What he did was unspeakable.
I was-- three occasions, I was so close to him as I am from you on three occasions.
He came to that camp.
He picked, at one time, close to 2,000 people, took them in the woods, shot them all.
Then he took 18 people from camp with wood to burn them.
They were burning day and night till they burned all the bodies.
Pardon me?
The living conditions in that camp was barracks-- big barracks-- also about 300 people are living in a barrack.
And the food was very, very little-- very little food.
The work was what we had to-- sorted merchandise.
What that was in that camp-- they brought in all furniture and all the belongings from all the Jewish people from the ghettos and from the little towns.
Everything what they had, they brought it into that camp.
And at that camp, we sorted, you know, the clothes, pants, you know, the sizes and things like this.
And that all was [INAUDIBLE] together.
It was transferred to Germany for the German population.
We had over there in that camp everything.
Whatever your heart desired, you had in that camp.
But on thing what we didn't have is our freedom and to eat.
This what we didn't have.
When I came to that camp, they ask everybody what kind of work he's doing, what kind of occupation.
So I told them I'm a tailor, so of course they put me into the tailor shop.
When I came into the tailor shop, I started working.
I got my machine, my table to cut and design.
Now, I worked over there, and I had to fit the Gestapo and SS.
When I fit them, my hands were shaking.
I felt that I cannot be responsible to be close to that people, to that kind of people, because of what they did and what they're doing.
I was afraid that I would lose my mind, I'd take a pair of scissors and cut some of their throat.
And it's not only that I would get killed, but that they would take 10, 20 people and kill.
I didn't want to have this on my conscience.
So I worked over there another couple days, and I ask over there to the people, whatever were responsible for it, to give me a transfer.
And just my luck-- it had to be that way-- they needed 10 boys to be in the cleanup group.
In German, they called it the [SPEAKING GERMAN]
That is to take the trashes from the camp.
They gave us a pair of horses with the buggy for two people, put it in the trashes and take them outside the camp.
That was about five, six kilometers outside the camp over there in the canyon to throw in the trashes.
We start working that.
So soon I came out.
But with every horse and buggy, one Ukrainian SS went with us.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
When we came out over there, there were Poles over there working also.
So I start talking to the Poles and ask them if they can bring me some food because we didn't have enough.
They say, yes, they can bring me some food, but how will we do it if the SS man-- the Ukrainian SS man is watching?
And I says, well, I will try to bribe them.
And I ask them what they need.
And they said, well, they needed pants.
They needed blankets.
They needed shoes.
They needed-- and I had this all just to take.
Put it in the trash, you know, and take it out.
So finally, you know, right next time I would start going, I start talking to the Ukrainian.
I know the language.
And I ask him what he needs.
He needs money.
He needs a gold watch.
He needs a diamond or a ring, whatever.
I can give it to him.
And I bribed him.
When I bribed him, I told him, whatever I do, you just look away.
You smoke a cigarette a little bit further, and I will talk to the Poles over there.
And I told him why I want to bring in some food.
I didn't want-- I told him that I am taking out merchandise.
Just-- he didn't know that.
OK, I start bringing in food.
And I said to the other buggies, because there was four more horse and buggies, you know, with boys going out with the same thing-- I told them what to do.
Not all of them could talk the language to the Ukrainians, you know, to bribe them.
So I went in their places, you know, and I used to bribe all the Ukrainians.
I talked to them.
I bribed them all when we start doing the business.
But we were bringing in food for the whole camp.
Every evening, each person, you know, had a barrack to supply the food.
You know, so there were five, you know.
And that's what we did.
We'd give a piece of bread.
[BACKGROUND NOISE]
OK, so when we were giving the food-- to make this story shorter-- this was several months that I was bribing the SS.
One particular time, the-- one SS man was riding on a horse when I was coming back with my horse and buggy, and it was loaded with sacks.
And he goes over there, sticks in his stick from the horse, and just happened that it was a sack of butter.
So he says, you know, what is this?
He took us right to the gallow over there to be hung, me and my friend.
And the Ukrainian SS, he put him to jail that he let us do this.
And in that camp, they did not hang by the neck.
They hang by the hand.
So we had-- we were tied up by the hand, and we had to be hung.
But he had to report this to his superior.
In the meantime, he made an appell.
That means a gathering for all the people in the camp.
They had to watch when we get hung, because if somebody was punished, all the people had to watch.
So when they made the appell, the commandant for the camp and the lageralteste, and all the big shots came, and they see me over there.
They know what I am doing, you know, why?
So five people-- it was the commandant, the lageralteste, his vice-president [INAUDIBLE], and the commandant from the [GERMAN] with his second man, and my kapo.
Five people went up to the hauptsturmfuhrer-- he was in charge of the camp-- and asked them for our lives.
So he told them, how come?
We hung here so many people.
We shot so many people, and you never put your life to ask for, you know, for someone else's life, just for them because they got the [SPEAKING GERMAN].
That means got the black market, you know, what we're making.
So the lageralteste, he was a very nice person, tall person.
His name was Krakauer.
And he was from Krakow, and his name was Krakauer.
He said, no, that's not true.
They're not the [SPEAKING GERMAN], because they are the people that are giving the food to all the people in the camp.
We think these people really can't do the work for you because we don't have enough to eat.
And thanks to these people that we can do the work.
And they're not taking any money from nobody, because they don't need any money.
And he asked the other ones, is that the truth?
And they said, yes, that's the truth.
Jawohl, hauptsturmfuhrer.
So when they said this, so he checked with his [INAUDIBLE] and told to his assistant or another officer, he says, go down and give them 25 lashes, and let them live.
So we got 25 lashes, and they let us live.
To make the story shorter, I-- that stopped for a few days.
I didn't went, but later I had to go, because people need to eat.
People, you know, were waiting for the food.
So I start working again like this, and we did that till the evacuation.
The evacuation of that camp-- we were over there 5,500 people in that camp.
This was in 1943. 5,500 plus-- men, women, and children.
We evacuated the camp.
They said 50 people will stay in the camp to dismantle the camp.
So all the prominent people stayed in the camp, all the [NON-ENGLISH] with the their kapos, with all the [NON-ENGLISH].
And there were 48 people.
They needed two more people.
They came to me.
They want me to stay in the camp with them.
They said, we'll have here very good.
We'll have enough to eat here, only 50 people of us, so it will be much easier-- and blah, blah, blah-- to stay with them.
So-- and I was always that person to see what for more people.
So I told them, you know what, Mr.
Krakauer, you can be here without me.
You have to take somebody else because, you see, all the people here are stepped out, and I see all the people lined up, men, women, and children.
So I said to him, maybe these people will need me more than you need me, because you won't need me here.
And I went with all the people.
When we came to the train, was wagons prepared.
Put in 110, 120 people in a wagon.
But before they put us into the train, we had to take off our clothes, only the pants-- our coat, our shirts, our shoes, our shoes, our socks, everything-- only left us in the pants.
And they took our clothes back to they camp.

When they took our clothes back to the camp, they recognize our clothes, so they thought that did to us the same thing what they did to the thousands of people before, where they shot them and burned them.
So a panic start.
So there were over there two mechanics that were fixing the cars for the Gestapo and for the SS.
So they fixed themselves a car, and they ran way.
When the two ran away, they took all the 48 people, and they shot them.
That goes to show you if I would be selfish, and I stay with them, I wouldn't be talking to you now.
But I wasn't selfish, I went with [INAUDIBLE].
They took us to Birkenau-Auschwitz.
On the road, we recognized where we were-- where we're going.
People started jumping from the train.
I told them, you know, there's nothing where to jump.
You know, it is so many things to talk, but such a long time.
When we came to Birkenau-Auschwitz--
It took us about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a day and a night.
We came to Birkenau-Auschwitz-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] well, a little bit more, because we left late in the evening, and we came over there late at night.
So it took about 30 hours to come to Birkenau.
When we came over there, Dr.
Mengele was waiting for us.
And right away, you know, left and right, left and right, where they took half of the people, put into the crematoriums.
And half of us went into the camp.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The people that went in the camp was young people.
But they had to-- we had to tell them to trade that we have.
If somebody didn't have a trade, you know, he had a very slim chance to let them through.
When we came in the camp, over there was the blockalteste.
And he was right away start beating us to lined up there, lined up there.
And he told us, do you think you came here to live?
Look over there at the crematoriums, how they are burning.
Over there you will go tomorrow.
They gave us that kind of welcome.
Right next, day, we had to take off our clothes, whatever we had, depends-- And they started looking if we have money or gold.
If somebody had golden teeth, they knocked them out.
I had in my hand folded together a picture of my parents.
An SS man seen that.
He thought that I have money.
He came, opened my hand.
There was the picture.
He tore the picture in pieces, and he beat me in the head with a stick.
I fell flat on my face.
I don't know how long I laid there, but I didn't know nothing.
Later, my friends picked me up.
And I was all bloody.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had to stand outside.
It was cold.
In the cold weather, we had to stand for hours till they would let us into the shower.
First, they shaved our bodies all over, put us into the shower, come out from the shower.
We had to stay naked.
It was cold outside.
The only warmth that we had was we got together, you know, and body warm.
And later we got our numbers.
The number is 161065.
[PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS]
That's what we were called.
We wasn't called by the names, just by the number.
And after the numbers, we had to stay, again, naked outside till they shaved our heads.
And they give us the clothes, what was wooden shoes, with [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] thin clothes, that-- clothes what you know from the camp, whatever we had.
And stay outside again till they brought soup for everybody till they get a little bit of soup.
And that you have to be very careful to say in a line.
If somebody wanted to push you or something, got beaten over the head right away.
So it wasn't worth it.
So just had to stay in the line till you could-- you come out and get a little bit of soup.
And they put us in in the barracks, was already late at night.
In the barracks, that was quarantined-- the quarantine camp.
We were there for six weeks in the quarantine.
Everyday, we had to be outside standing, and they were counting us.
I don't know-- we didn't have no place where to go.
I don't know why they were counting every day, several times a day.
Every hour, every half hour, they come, and they count.
And we had to stay outside the whole day in that freezing weather.
It was just a terrible way to live.
But we had to think of tomorrow and not to give up.
Finally, after the six weeks, they took us to the Auschwitz.
In Auschwitz, I was there a few days.
And from Auschwitz, we went to Buna Auschwitz.
